title: April 2024
excerpt: Learning Java and the beginning of the journey.
content: I started learning Java, to be better at work. My plan at the time was to try to become very good in my profession. Learn an amazing field, get better jobs, or potentially build something of my own.

title: October 2024
excerpt: Building a chess engine and wanting projects that tell a story.
content:
After I learned the fundamentals in Java, and after exploring a Java framework at work, I decided it's time to make a serious project. Build up a portfolio. Since I enjoy chess, I started building a chess engine.

In the following months, I spent some time after work on it and I began to find the project more and more interesting. I was increasingly convinced that from now on I want to work on projects like this one. Projects that are interesting but at the same time more personal, and can describe a part of my personality, like enjoying chess.

title: February 2025
excerpt: Turning the chess engine into a full chess web application.
content: If a project tells a part of my personality, I want people to experience it the way I communicate it. I decided to turn the chess engine, previously run locally, into a full chess web application. I spend the next months building it with great enthusiasm.

title: April 2025
excerpt: First move over websockets, UI, and learning that appearance and story matter.
content:
For the first time, I had a move sent over WebSockets to the other player and displayed in the frontend. I was very happy to see it working, and next I wanted to shape its appearance to communicate my aesthetic preferences.

This part took much longer than I expected. While working on it, I realized that the things I value most tend to be expressed through the visual experience of the site. Even though this is just a personal project, it revolves around chess, something I deeply appreciate, and the implementation behind it was quite complex. Because of that, I wanted the frontend, the part people experience most directly, to feel intentional as well and describe another part of myself. In a way, the project reflects three different layers: my appreciation for chess itself, the programming work behind the scenes, and finally the visual experience that anyone encountering the site will feel, even if they know nothing about me or the months I spent building it. I wanted visiting the site to create a specific atmosphere, almost like a small narrative, expressed through the header content, the colors, the music, the quotes, and the overall presentation.

Working on this, I realized something that felt like a turning point for me. I understood that I want the things I build to express my personal preferences, and for people to experience them and connect with them. A chess app naturally connects with anyone who enjoys chess. Programming a chess app connects with people who appreciate the craft of building software. But the aesthetic experience of the site connects in a more abstract way. In that sense, the project becomes a kind of story as it presents itself to the user.

I realized that I enjoy presenting things in this way: creating projects that communicate a viewpoint and invite people into an experience. Visiting the site is meant to feel intentional — something made with care and purpose. Not for commercial reasons, not for profit, and not simply to demonstrate technical ability, but to share something that was lived in and shaped over months of building it.

Realizing this changed the way I think about my work as a software engineer too. Of course, I am very proud of my work. I find great satisfaction in it, I respect it as a field, and I enjoy working with people who share that passion, people who are honest, collaborative, and committed to producing work that is exceptional at doing what it is meant to do. I enjoy this a lot.

Most professional software, though, exists to serve a clear purpose and ultimately create value for a business. Even the chess app I built, if used widely, would be judged as a good online chess app, and fairly so. The narrative and story it tells, which are what I am most passionate about, would be just an extra.

I realized that I want to work on things that are about more than functionality, things that create a narrative you can step into. Not something meant to sell, but something that exists for itself, to be experienced by anyone willing to engage with it, much like we do when we commit to watching a film.

From April until June, I continued refining SKGCHESS to match the way I had imagined it. During this period, it was used primarily by friends and colleagues, as well as at work-related demonstrations, giving people the opportunity to experience the project firsthand.

title: June 2025
excerpt: Two paths (game development or freelancing), first game concept, and the chess club email.
content:
It was time to move on from SKGCHESS. Having understood that it is a personal project that attempted to tell a story to those who visited it, I started thinking in ways that I could continue viewing my work from this perspective, but creating something that people would want to be more invested in. SKGCHESS was, at the end of the day, a good personal project. For the reason it was created, even I would use chess.com or another more complete popular site.

I had two ways:

  • Game development
  • Freelancing (trying out more projects like this where mainly I can express aesthetics but not ideas. Mostly small projects, maybe in an area where I help someone).

Mid June 2025

The First Game Concept

Having always liked playing games or watching movies, I knew from the moment I started studying Computer Science in university, that game development could be a career I pursue. Specifically solo game development. Before June 2025, I had already tried to play with a game engine, the first time being in winter in 2023, where I just messed up with things, and the second in February of 2025, spending a few days. It's worth noting that the model of the dog appearing in the front page, was made during those days. It is my dog Lilith, as you can see here :)

(image of lilith)

Both times, I thought about game development and tried to examine it but rejected the option. In 2023, I did so because I believed I should first pursue software engineering and try to build a good career in it, and the second time because I was still coming to terms with all the ideas I described earlier.

This third time, I did not open up any engine. Since expression is what I care about, I told myself: if I figure out that I want to tell a story and it has meaning for me, then how to tell the story comes second.

So I wrote down an idea for a scenario. Very roughly: you are inside the mind of a protagonist who is in a state of dormancy, and you are asked to explore its different regions. Each region represents a lost emotion. Afterwards, I started thinking about gameplay mechanics around this idea. For example, the player could have a weapon that fires emotions, and depending on the emotion it shoots, it has different effects on the environment. There are many more ideas, but it's probably better not to expand too much here. I found myself being more attracted, though, to the atmosphere and feeling of the game. I would like something cinematic, thought-provoking, handling existential questions, or philosophizing, while also being fun to play.

Next, I decided that it would be wise to better understand what a game actually is. So I read a book about game design named "A Theory of Fun for Game Design" by Raph Koster. I took away many interesting insights from the book and continued thinking through what I want to create, and whether making a game is the right choice for it.

End June 2025

I received an email about skgchess. I had posted it on LinkedIn some time ago, and a friend from work approached me to help. The whole discussion pushed me to return to the project again and "perfect" it in order to post it when I would be ready, in the website of one of the few local chess clubs in my hometown, Thessaloniki.

title: August 2025
excerpt: Launching IXLSTUDIO as a freelancing page.
content:
SKGCHESS was almost ready for the local chess club. All this effort pushed me to think about starting freelancing, something that is for sure much easier than creating a game.

I launched IXLSTUDIO as my freelancing page. The idea was for people that visit SKGCHESS, to check on IXLSTUDIO and my work too.

I spent the rest of the month and early september, experimenting with the design of the IXLSTUDIO.

title: September 2025
excerpt: Taking a step back, Unreal Engine, painting, Blender, FL Studio, and the big questions.
content:
After returning from vacation, I decided it was time to take a step back.

I had now spent more than seven months of what constant exploration. I needed to slow down a bit.

After resting for some time, the idea of posting SKGCHESS and freelancing was now something I was not so sure I wanted to continue.

I decided to try game development again, this time just following a tutorial in Unreal Engine. Since my PC could not run the engine smoothly, I rented the Shadow PC service. In the next days, I created some landscapes and played with assets. It was actually really creative.

With this, I started being curious about art in general (if I were to make a game I would like to create my own art in a way). I began painting for the first time. Also, I read a book that a good friend gifted to me, "The Story of Art" by Gombrich, and started experimenting with Blender, as well as FLStudio for Music.

I would then spent the next months being involved from time to time with these tools. I would frequently though come back and forth in thinking about the idea of creating a game and having the following questions.

Do I really want to make a game? Is the game the right medium to be expressed? If so, What kind of game I would like to make? And what kind of game I can realistically make?

title: January 2026
excerpt: Writing the complete story and the world document.
content:
I came back to what I did in June with the scenario attempt and this time I tried harder. I wrote a complete story, a mixed version of my first scenario, along with new direction. I made a document with the entire history of the world: how it could be delivered narratively, what type of game could help achieve the message, and how the gameplay mechanics could connect with the story of the world.

Knowing that I had a story to tell, the questions that still remained were: What type of game? 3D or 2D? Platformer or shooter? For example, I wanted to make an atmospheric game, perhaps a cinematic one, with clever mechanics. Each desired result is tied to specific genres and perspectives.

The decision between 3D and 2D would also affect the choice of game engine, as Unreal is generally recommended for 3D and Unity for 2D.

title: February 2026
excerpt: Switching to open source and looking into Godot.
content: After moving away from Spotify, I decide more broadly that I want to avoid proprietary software. I switched fully to open source. I reinstalled Linux (which I had used as a student), purged windows, and with it Shadow PC service too. With this shift, I decide that if I would make a game, it should also be in an open source engine. So I started looking into Godot.

title: March 2026
excerpt: Starting the game in Godot and Day 1 with the documentation.
content:
The journey of almost one year of constant searching feels like it is finally coming to an end. Along the way, since many of these questions were existential, I changed my perspective on things many times. I imagine that every few years in a person's life, something like this happens: a new perspective appears, and a new chapter begins.

I decided I will make a game in Godot, based on the scenario I have written. I don't want to overthink it anymore, so I will simply start with a 3D first-person game, mainly because I want 3D, and first-person avoids the animation and camera complexity that third-person requires.

A few days ago, I finished ixlstudio in the form you see now. At the same time, I also started teaching some classes related to my profession at a college. My daily job continues to go well. It's a good period where the work of the past year starts coming together as this new project begins.

We start with Day 1.
>Go to the journal to start following the documentation of making the game.
